meta.sermon_title: It Still Works | Pastor Steven Furtick | Elevation Church

summary: Furtick preaches a 50-minute motivational exposition of Exodus 4, repeating the refrain "It still works" roughly forty times. The through-line: Moses doubted, delayed, and complained but God used him anyway — therefore the listener's doubts, delays, and bad attitudes do not disqualify them. The sermon's signature move is a prop bit where Furtick plays a 26-year-old cassette of his own preaching through a refurbished boombox to argue that "the method changes but the message is the same." He closes with a testimony of a man in Greenville, SC, who (per Furtick) set down a 9mm after hearing a Furtick podcast sermon. The sermon is largely apolitical and contains no identifiable Christian-nationalist, anti-Muslim, anti-LGBTQ, or anti-Jewish rhetoric. The main analytical concerns are (1) a formulaic "it works if you work it" framing of faith that edges toward mechanism/prosperity, (2) a flippant aside about child discipline ("at least you didn't kill them… God will fix it"), (3) a monetized-giving pitch framed as "weird but it works," and (4) handling of suicidal ideation as something to "do doubting" without referral to care.

axis_1.label: Fidelity to Jesus's Teachings (Red Letters)
axis_1.headline: Three flagged items — a formulaic 'it works if you work it' framing sits in tension with Jesus's Gethsemane prayer and Matt 26:39; flippant 'at least you didn't kill them' about children cuts against Matt 18:6; political aside is fleeting.

axis_2.label: Identifiable Biases / Harmful Rhetoric
axis_2.headline: No identifiable CN markers beyond a one-line 'politics' aside. No anti-Muslim, anti-LGBTQ, anti-Jewish, or misogynistic framing. One low-severity concern: unprompted mention of suicidal ideation ('do it with suicidal thoughts, but do it') is pastorally thin — meant to encourage but delivered without any referral to care.

axis_3.label: Factual Claims & Evidence Check
axis_3.verdict: Light on empirical claims; biblical citations largely accurate
axis_3.headline: The sermon is mostly exhortation, not argument from facts. Biblical chronology claims (430 years in Egypt; 40 years in Midian; Moses' name etymology) are either biblically supported or reflect the Bible's own folk-etymology. One testimony (Greenville suicide intervention via podcast) is unverifiable by design.

axis_4.label: Whole-Bible Engagement
axis_4.verdict: Narrow lane, minor cherry-pick on suffering
axis_4.headline: Furtick preaches one book (Exodus) consistently but narrates faith as mechanism ('it works if you work it') without engaging the substantial canonical witness — Job, Ecclesiastes, the Psalms of lament, Jesus in Gethsemane, Heb 11:35–39 — where faith emphatically does not 'work' in the sense the sermon implies.

tenets_affirmed: Exposition of Exodus 4 tracks the text accurately: Moses' objection (4:1), the staff-to-snake sign (4:2–4), the two-handed divine assurance (4:17), and the return to Egypt (4:19–20) are all rendered faithfully.

Acknowledges doubt, fear, and bad attitudes as normal rather than disqualifying in the life of faith — consonant with biblical figures from Moses (Ex 4) to Thomas (John 20) to the father in Mark 9:24.
Correctly identifies Paul and Silas's midnight praise in the 'inner cell' (Acts 16:24–25) and the pattern of worship preceding deliverance.
Repeatedly tells hearers who feel suicidal or worthless to 'live another day' and 'take another breath' — pastoral affirmation of life.
counter_signals: No political party, candidate, or policy is named or targeted from the pulpit; the lone political reference ('I wish we could preach some of that in politics today') is a throwaway aside without partisan direction.
No anti-Muslim, anti-LGBTQ, anti-Jewish, or misogynistic framing detected in the sermon.

Self-deprecating posture throughout — Furtick names his own long-running preaching anxiety ('my hands were shaking while I held my pen'; 'I didn't know anything I was talking about') rather than posturing as a prophetic strongman.
Affirms life directly to listeners with suicidal ideation ('live another day'; 'take another breath').
Explicit multi-campus inclusion (Houston shout-out; YouTube audience from named states) without ethno-cultural 'real American' coding.
Acknowledges that past methods (tapes, CDs, Campobello small-church preaching) were not inferior to current platform reach — a non-triumphalist note about church growth.

findings.title: Faith as mechanism: 'it works if you work it'
findings.pastor_quote: And it works if you work it… Won't it work? I said, 'Won't it work?' Not if you don't work it… It'll work when you're old. It'll work in this season. It'll work when they like you. It'll work when they hate you. It'll work when they lie about you. It'll work when they leave you.

jesus_quotes.text: My Father, if it be possible, let this cup pass from me; nevertheless, not as I will, but as you will.
Your kingdom come, your will be done, on earth as it is in heaven.

jesus_quotes.citation: John 11:21, 32 (Martha and Mary to Jesus about Lazarus)
jesus_quotes.text: Lord, if you had been here, my brother would not have died.

jesus_quotes.citation: Mark 15:34 — Jesus citing Ps 22:1 from the cross
jesus_quotes.text: Eloi, Eloi, lema sabachthani?' which means, 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?'

findings.why_flagged: Jesus's own model of faith culminates in a prayer that is not answered in the way the petitioner requests — the cup does not pass — and in a cry of forsakenness quoting a Hebrew psalm of lament. A refrain insisting that prayer/faith/praise 'still works' if you 'work it' — extended across every domain the listener might bring — sits uneasily against the canonical picture in which the Son himself prays a prayer that does not 'work' in those terms, precisely so that something larger than mechanism can happen.

findings.title: Flippant aside about child discipline
findings.pastor_quote: Well, at least you didn't kill them. I mean, at least they're still here. The Bible says if you train up a child in the way they should go, when they're old they won't depart from it. That means all you've got to do is not kill them, and God will fix it. In the meantime, just let them live.

jesus_quotes.text: Whoever causes one of these little ones who believe in me to sin, it would be better for him to have a great millstone fastened around his neck and to be drowned in the depth of the sea.
Let the little children come to me and do not hinder them, for to such belongs the kingdom of heaven.
Whoever causes one of these little ones who believe in me to sin, it would be better for him if a great millstone were hung around his neck and he were thrown into the sea.

findings.why_flagged: Aimed as a comedic laugh-line about parental blow-ups, the framing sets a floor ('not killing' your kids) so low that it functionally dismisses the range of harms short of homicide that Jesus treats as grave. Matt 18:6 sets a drastically different calibration. The joke also misappropriates Prov 22:6 as a guarantee (it is a proverb, not a warranty) to relieve the parent of responsibility for the 'blew up at the kids' behavior just invoked. Low-to-moderate severity pastoral miscalibration to a mass audience likely to include survivors of parental abuse.

findings.title: Tithing/giving framed as 'weird but it works' mechanism
findings.pastor_quote: It's weird for us to give to God so that our church can do outreaches, so that our church can reach the world, so that our church can preach the gospel. That is weird, but you find out that God doesn't need what you have; you need what he has when you give, and it allows you to access your faith… people are like, 'You give your money to that church?' 'No. I give it to God through a church.'… So that's weird, but it works.

jesus_quotes.text: When you give to the needy, sound no trumpet before you, as the hypocrites do in the synagogues and in the streets, that they may be praised by others… But when you give to the needy, do not let your left hand know what your right hand is doing.

And he sat down opposite the treasury and watched the people putting money into the offering box. Many rich people put in large sums… she out of her poverty has put in everything she had, all she had to live on.
For I was hungry and you gave me food, I was thirsty and you gave me drink, I was a stranger and you welcomed me… as you did it to one of the least of these my brothers, you did it to me.

findings.why_flagged: Jesus's teaching on giving is overwhelmingly directed toward the poor, the hungry, the stranger, and 'the least of these' — not toward institutional maintenance framed in the possessive ('our church'). Using the sermon's central 'it works' mechanism to authenticate tithing to the preaching institution itself is low-severity but notable. The Gospel counter-witness would route the mechanism outward (the poor) rather than inward (the church organization reaching 'the world' through its platform).

findings.title: Suicidal ideation addressed without referral to care
findings.pastor_quote: Do it doubting, but do it. Do it afraid, but do it. Do it with suicidal thoughts, but do it. Live another day thinking you don't deserve to be on the earth, but live another day. Take another breath feeling anxious, but take another breath.

jesus_quotes.text: Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest. Take my yoke upon you, and learn from me, for I am gentle and lowly in heart, and you will find rest for your souls.

jesus_quotes.citation: Luke 10:34 (the Good Samaritan — actively transporting a wounded stranger to a place of care)
jesus_quotes.text: He went to him and bound up his wounds, pouring on oil and wine. Then he set him on his own animal and brought him to an inn and took care of him.

findings.why_flagged: The intent is pastoral affirmation — 'stay alive' — and is counted here as a counter-signal as well. But addressing an audience including people with active suicidal ideation from a mega-church pulpit and reducing the prescription to 'do it anyway' — without naming 988, a counseling referral, or any non-'work it yourself' route — is pastorally thin. Low severity because the directional effect is pro-life, but flagged so that the sermon's mass reach gets the benefit of the fuller canonical posture (Matt 11) in which the heavily-laden are pointed toward help, not just told to push through.

findings.title: Political aside conflating church unity with partisan discourse
findings.pastor_quote: I wish we could preach some of that in politics today. I wish we could preach some of that in the church today. 'You're supposed to be on the same side. Really? Y'all are fighting?'

jesus_quotes.text: That they may all be one, just as you, Father, are in me, and I in you, that they also may be in us, so that the world may believe that you have sent me.

So if you are offering your gift at the altar and there remember that your brother has something against you, leave your gift there before the altar and go. First be reconciled to your brother, and then come and offer your gift.
Teacher, tell my brother to divide the inheritance with me.' But he said to him, 'Man, who made me a judge or arbitrator over you?'

findings.why_flagged: Very low-severity finding — a single aside, not a theme. But the sentence assumes 'we' (Christians, or Americans) are all 'supposed to be on the same side,' a framing that flattens genuine moral disagreement into a unity-against-other posture. Jesus's own pattern with unity language is intra-ecclesial (John 17), not civic, and he repeatedly declines the role of civic arbitrator (Luke 12). Included here for coverage, not because it drives the sermon.

axis4_findings.title: Faith as 'it works' — mechanism without the canonical lament tradition
axis4_findings.topic: What faith looks like when it doesn't produce the outcome sought
axis4_findings.pastor_quote: Faith still works. Faith still works. Grace still works. Gratitude still works. 'Hallelujah' still works. 'Thank you, Jesus' still works. It still works!… It'll get you through the dry season. It'll get you through the panic attack. It'll get you through it, because it still works.

jesus_passages.text: My soul is very sorrowful, even to death; remain here, and watch with me… My Father, if it be possible, let this cup pass from me; nevertheless, not as I will, but as you will.

jesus_passages.citation: Mark 15:34 — Jesus citing Ps 22:1 from the cross
jesus_passages.text: My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?
When Jesus saw her weeping… he was deeply moved in his spirit and greatly troubled… Jesus wept.
Do you think that these Galileans were worse sinners than all the other Galileans, because they suffered in this way? No, I tell you… unless you repent, you will all likewise perish.

wider_bible_passages.text: Behold, I go forward, but he is not there, and backward, but I do not perceive him; on the left hand when he is working, I do not behold him; he turns to the right hand, but I do not see him.

Again I saw that under the sun the race is not to the swift, nor the battle to the strong, nor bread to the wise, nor riches to the intelligent, nor favor to those with knowledge, but time and chance happen to them all.

wider_bible_passages.citation: Ps 88:18 (a psalm of lament that ends without resolution)
wider_bible_passages.text: You have caused my beloved and my friend to shun me; my companions have become darkness.

Some were tortured, refusing to accept release, so that they might rise again to a better life. Others suffered mocking and flogging, and even chains and imprisonment. They were stoned, they were sawn in two, they were killed with the sword… And all these, though commended through their faith, did not receive what was promised.
Three times I pleaded with the Lord about this, that it should leave me. But he said to me, 'My grace is sufficient for you, for my power is made perfect in weakness.'

For I consider that the sufferings of this present time are not worth comparing with the glory that is to be revealed to us… For we know that the whole creation has been groaning together in the pains of childbirth until now. And not only the creation, but we ourselves… groan inwardly.

axis4_findings.analysis: The biblical canon contains a substantial tradition — Job, Ecclesiastes, roughly one third of the Psalter (the laments), Lamentations, and Hebrews 11:35–39 — that is precisely about faith when it does not 'work' in the sense the sermon invokes. Jesus himself prays a Gethsemane prayer that does not 'work' (the cup does not pass) and quotes Ps 22's opening line of forsakenness from the cross. Paul is denied three times when he asks for the thorn's removal. Hebrews 11, the canonical faith chapter, ends by insisting that some faithful people were 'stoned… sawn in two… killed with the sword' and 'did not receive what was promised.' Furtick's refrain — extended to panic attacks, dry seasons, enemies, leavers — isn't heretical; prayer, worship, and praise are valuable regardless of outcome. But by framing faith exclusively through 'it works' he leaves the listener without the canonical vocabulary for the time when, by any ordinary human reckoning, it doesn't. No reference to Gethsemane. No reference to the laments. No reference to Hebrews 11:35–39. The narrow lane is the Moses-gets-the-deliverance arc, generalized into a formula.
axis4_findings.why_flagged: The sermon's load-bearing refrain is a formula ('it works if you work it') that the canon explicitly and repeatedly qualifies — including in Jesus's own prayer life. A mass-audience pastor can affirm that God is faithful without adopting a mechanism frame the canon itself resists.

axis4_findings.title: Doubt reframed as fuel — but without the canonical warnings
axis4_findings.topic: How scripture evaluates persistent doubt and divided mind
axis4_findings.pastor_quote: The first point of my message is sometimes you've got to do it doubting… Sometimes great faith is not eliminating doubt; it is looking at doubt right in the face and telling doubt, 'You can sit in the passenger seat, but you can't drive, because we're doing this.'

jesus_passages.text: All things are possible for one who believes.' Immediately the father of the child cried out and said, 'I believe; help my unbelief!'
O you of little faith, why did you doubt?
Put your finger here, and see my hands; and put out your hand, and place it in my side. Do not disbelieve, but believe.
And when they saw him they worshiped him, but some doubted.

wider_bible_passages.text: But let him ask in faith, with no doubting, for the one who doubts is like a wave of the sea that is driven and tossed by the wind. For that person must not suppose that he will receive anything from the Lord; he is a double-minded man, unstable in all his ways.
And have mercy on those who doubt.
But whoever has doubts is condemned if he eats, because the eating is not from faith. For whatever does not proceed from faith is sin.
For we walk by faith, not by sight.

axis4_findings.analysis: The canon is genuinely in tension on doubt. Jesus's pastoral posture with Thomas, with the 'I believe; help my unbelief' father, and with the Matt 28:17 worshipers-who-doubted is accommodating and gentle — he accepts the mixed state and works within it. That matches Furtick's point well. But the other canonical voice — James 1:6–8 in particular — is sharp: the double-minded person 'must not suppose that he will receive anything from the Lord.' Paul in Rom 14:23 is also sharp. Furtick is not wrong to highlight the Jesus-with-Thomas strand; he is on solid Gospel ground. The gap is that he doesn't acknowledge that the wider canon contains a harsher counter-voice — which, notably, lives inside the very mechanism ('ask in faith and you will receive') the sermon's 'it works' refrain is extending. If the mechanism is right, James's warning about the double-minded is also right. Furtick wants the mechanism and the doubt-permission simultaneously; the canon makes you pay for that by at least naming the tension.
axis4_findings.why_flagged: The sermon draws heavily from the doubt-accommodating Gospel strand (which is real) while adopting an 'it works if you work it' mechanism that the doubt-warning strand (James 1:6–8; Rom 14:23) explicitly gates behind undivided faith. Either strand is defensible; the combination needs the tension named.

factual_claims.claim: The people of Israel had been enslaved for four centuries (430 years) in Egypt.
factual_claims.evidence_cited_by_pastor: None explicitly; delivered as a biblical aside.
factual_claims.analysis: This is directly supported by the Hebrew Bible's own chronology: Exodus 12:40 states, 'The time that the people of Israel lived in Egypt was 430 years.' Genesis 15:13 also gives a round number of 400 years for the affliction. (Scholarly/historical dating of the events is contested, but Furtick's claim is a biblical-chronology claim, and on its own terms the text supports it.)

factual_claims.claim: Moses' name means 'drawn out of water.'
factual_claims.evidence_cited_by_pastor: None; delivered as a biblical fact.
factual_claims.analysis: This is the folk-etymology the Bible itself gives in Exodus 2:10: Pharaoh's daughter 'named him Moses, saying, "Because I drew him out of the water."' However, most contemporary Egyptologists and Hebrew Bible scholars note that the name is more likely Egyptian in origin, related to the root 'msj' meaning 'to give birth to' or 'son' — as in Thutmose ('son of Thoth') or Ramesses ('son of Ra'). The Hebrew verb māšâ ('to draw out') is a plausible Hebrew folk etymology overlaid on an Egyptian name. Furtick's claim tracks the biblical text's own self-interpretation; it is supported on the Bible's terms and partially supported on scholarly terms.

factual_claims.claim: Paul and Silas were fastened in the inner cell — 'the hardest place to get out of' — and they sang at midnight.
factual_claims.evidence_cited_by_pastor: None cited; delivered as a biblical aside.
factual_claims.analysis: Directly supported by Acts 16:24–25: 'Having received this order, he put them into the inner prison and fastened their feet in the stocks. About midnight Paul and Silas were praying and singing hymns to God, and the prisoners were listening to them.' The characterization is faithful to the text.

factual_claims.claim: A man in Greenville, SC, with a 9mm handgun on his coffee table during a divorce, plugged in an Apple TV his mother had brought over, clicked a purple 'Podcast' icon, then an orange 'Elevation' icon, and heard a Furtick sermon called 'Handle It' preached ten years prior; he subsequently became a worship leader at a local church.
factual_claims.evidence_cited_by_pastor: Furtick attributes it to 'a testimony I heard the other day' from the man himself. No name, no date, no independent documentation offered.
factual_claims.analysis: This is an uncorroborated pastoral testimony. Key unverifiable elements: (1) the man's identity is withheld, so the account cannot be checked; (2) the claimed Apple TV UI behavior (a purple 'Podcast' icon, a separate orange 'Elevation' icon) does not cleanly match the standard Apple TV interface — Apple's Podcasts app icon is purple, but there is no standalone 'Elevation' app in the Apple TV App Store that surfaces as a top-level colored tile to a user who has never used the platform before; Elevation content is typically distributed via the Podcasts app, YouTube, and third-party channels. (3) Furtick has preached material titled 'Handle It' touching Exodus 4, and the sermon frames 'It Still Works' as a callback to that earlier message, but I cannot independently confirm the exact 10-year delta or the testimony chain. What would need to be checked: the existence and date of the 'Handle It' sermon in Elevation's public archive; whether Elevation has a named/consented version of this testimony; and the plausibility of the UI description. The underlying pastoral point can stand on its own, but the specific event as narrated is not verifiable from public sources.

cn_markers.marker: A. Conflation of national & Christian identity
cn_markers.note: No America-as-chosen-nation rhetoric; no flag imagery; no national-favor framing.
Staff/snake/plague imagery is used exegetically for Moses, not transposed onto a present-day culture war.

cn_markers.marker: C. Political opponents as spiritual enemies

cn_markers.note: No persecution narrative; the sermon's adversary is private doubt, not cultural hostility.
Multi-state/multi-city YouTube roll call is geographically inclusive.

other_axis2_rubrics.note: A single chastity reference ('You're a virgin? That's weird. No. It works') is directed generally, not targeted at any identity group.
Passing 'God just deals with Jezebel' idiom is used of an external obstacle in the pastor's own story, not of women generally.
Exodus narrative is read as the Bible's own deliverance story; the typological reading of Jesus as 'our true Moses' (00:23:00) is Christological without denigrating Jewish identity.
